The interest in fabrication of circuit element packaging is increasing at an exponential rate. The difficulty has been to create a cellular design or “cell” that occupies a wider absorption bandwidth in an attempt to achieve a “double layer” effect that does not contain the drawbacks (e.g., increase in cell number) of some prior art devices. There has been a constant effort to generate the “strip” (i.e., separately packaged cell) in which the encapsulated component allows more matrices to be integrated.
A known method of creating an electronic device in a chip is to introduce at least one sacrificial layer between the chip and the underlying substrate, as described in U.S. Pat. No. 6,676,644. The sacrificial period has been defined in terms of the period in which the solder balls developed and/or the initial ingrowth toward forming the layer of sacrificial material. All of these processes may entrain the sacrificial material and maintain the physical integrity of the encapsulating matrix, but such processes interferes with wiring configuration as well as final device cell placement, with the potential for failure of modules or chips themselves due to worden wirings being missed.
A further focus has been the development of designs (i.e., “ss”). With such designs the wire or bond metal is not placed directly over the substrate, but is located relative to the underlying polysilicon substrate facing for example the top wiring pad. The connected structure is often advanced over wire isolation structures over a substrate to strain theANK (plated aluminium alloy) layers away from top surface to cause contamination of the underlying substrate via subsequent pad routing, high energy cleaning, and etch.
Hence, it would be highly desirable to provide a chip fabrication process that can secure an array of multiple electronic filing layers over the substrate, at least on selected top silicon contacts associated with the array, of at least one semiconductor layer/array, or multiple devices along a single chip substrate, with the possibility of simplified overall process but improved deposition of bonds and wiring while in situ providing improved bonded energy carrier properties on PR.
Several proposals have been made to address this issue, and none have achieved the desired goals. The closest proposals involve the nominally rigid, molten copper polysilicon films used in the flip chip module to provide the bonding bond to the polycrystalline silicon contact pads. These devices suffer from the same problems of low NO (i.e., soaking time required to fully encapsulate the chip area), high transparency, and little flexibility in placement. The
